key: hull|2017|putting on my best normal pmc5
bare: - Hull et al. (2017) -- "Putting on My Best Normal" (PMC5509825): Qualitative study of 92 autistic adults; camouflaging composed of assimilation, masking, and compensation (including social scripts); short- and long-term consequences include exhaustion and threats to self-perception
claim_context: Used to anchor the three-factor model of camouflaging (assimilation, masking, compensation) and its costs — exhaustion, identity threat — in 92 autistic adults.
source_says: Hull et al. (2017, JADD) qualitatively analyzed 92 autistic adults' experiences and identified assimilation, masking, and compensation as the core camouflaging components, with exhaustion, anxiety, and threats to self-perception as consequences.
claim_support: Direct: the paper is the foundational qualitative account the hub cites, and the three-factor description plus cost list come directly from its thematic analysis.

key: hull|2017|putting on my best normal soci
bare: Hull, L., Petrides, K. V., Allison, C., Smith, P., Baron-Cohen, S., Lai, M.-C., & Mandy, W. (2017). "Putting on my best normal": Social camouflaging in adults with autism spectrum conditions. *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 47(8), 2519-2534. [PMC5509825](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5509825/)
claim_context: Full bibliographic variant of Hull 2017 "Putting on My Best Normal," cited across ~28 files as the primary source for camouflaging framework and its costs.
source_says: Same paper as above — the seminal qualitative account of social camouflaging in autistic adults.
claim_support: Direct; this is the canonical citation for the camouflaging concept that the hub uses throughout.
notes: Housekeeping: duplicate of the PMC-only variant above; master dedupe will collapse.

bare: **Hull et al. (2017, PMC5509825)** introduced the CAT-Q (Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire) and documented that masking — the conscious modulation of communication and affect to appear less autistic — imposes substantial cognitive and emotional costs. **Hull et al. (2019)** deepened this with qualitative work on the reasons, contexts, and costs of camouflaging. **Cook et al. (2021)** conducted a systematic review that found camouflaging to be consistently associated with anxiety, depression, burnout, and the erosion of self-knowledge. [VALIDATED — one of the strongest consensus findings in contemporary autism research]
claim_context: Summary statement framing Hull 2019 as a qualitative deepening of the 2017 Hull paper — reasons, contexts, and costs of camouflaging.
source_says: Hull et al. (2019) "Understanding the Reasons, Contexts and Costs of Camouflaging" (JADD 49:1899-1911) is a thematic analysis of 262 autistic adults describing why they camouflage, in what situations, and at what cost.
claim_support: Direct: the 2019 paper explicitly frames itself as examining reasons/contexts/costs, exactly as the hub summarizes.
notes: Minor historical error in the hub's passage: CAT-Q was actually introduced in Hull et al. 2019, not 2017 — the 2017 paper is the qualitative precursor. The claim sentence should swap these.

bare: Hull, L., Mandy, W., Lai, M.-C., Baron-Cohen, S., Allison, C., Smith, P., & Petrides, K. V. (2019). Development and validation of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q). *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 49(3), 819-833. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6394785/)
urls: Hull et al. (2019) - Development and Validation of the CAT-Q
claim_context: Cited as the paper that developed and validated the CAT-Q — the standard self-report camouflaging measure.
source_says: Hull et al. (2019, JADD 49:819-833) developed and psychometrically validated the CAT-Q, yielding three subscales: compensation, masking, and assimilation.
claim_support: Direct and canonical: the paper's title and purpose are exactly the CAT-Q development and validation the hub cites.
notes: Minor: the first URL (PMC6394586) points to a different article; the correct PMC ID for Hull 2019 CAT-Q is PMC6394785. Housekeeping fix needed.

bare: - **Hull et al. (2019)** -- development of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q).
claim_context: Short-form citation for the CAT-Q development paper.
source_says: Same as above — Hull 2019 JADD CAT-Q validation.
claim_support: Direct: the CAT-Q is exactly what the paper develops.

bare: - Hyland, K. (1998). *Hedging in Scientific Research Articles*. Amsterdam: John Benjamins. [Publisher Page](https://benjamins.com/catalog/pbns.54)
claim_context: Cited as the foundational monograph on hedging in academic/scientific writing.
source_says: Hyland's 1998 Benjamins monograph developed the pragmatic account of epistemic hedging in scientific research articles, distinguishing content-oriented vs. reader-oriented hedges.
claim_support: Direct and canonical for hedging theory in written discourse.

bare: - Hyland, K. (2005). *Metadiscourse: Exploring Interaction in Writing*. New York: Continuum. [Hyland's Metadiscourse PDF](http://www.kenhyland.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Metadiscourse_What-is-it-and-where-is-it-going.pdf)
claim_context: Cited as a canonical source for the theory of metadiscourse and interactional hedges.
source_says: Hyland 2005 Metadiscourse proposes an interpersonal model of metadiscourse (interactive vs. interactional resources) with hedges as a core interactional category.
claim_support: Direct; the book is the standard theoretical reference for the hedge-metadiscourse link.

bare: - Hymes, D. (1972). "On Communicative Competence." In J.B. Pride & J. Holmes (eds.), *Sociolinguistics*. [Amazon (parent volume)](https://www.amazon.com/Sociolinguistics-Selected-Readings-J-B-Pride/dp/0140806652)
claim_context: Cited as the origin of "communicative competence" — competence beyond grammar into appropriate use.
source_says: Hymes's canonical 1972 essay defines communicative competence as knowing what to say, to whom, when, how, and what is appropriate — the foundation of modern pragmatics/sociolinguistics.

key: hyter|2017|the art of common ground emerg
bare: - [Hyter et al. (2017) - The art of common ground: emergence of a complex pragmatic skill in ASD](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4764348/)
urls: Hyter et al. (2017) - The art of common ground: emergence of a complex pragmatic skill in ASD
claim_context: Cited as the source showing that typically developing participants produce shorter narratives when sharing knowledge with an interlocutor — an effect absent at the group level in ASD.
source_says: PMC4764348 is actually de Marchena & Eigsti (2016) "The art of common ground: emergence of a complex pragmatic language skill in adolescents with autism spectrum disorders," Journal of Child Language 43(1):43-80. The content described is correct, but the authorship is wrong.
claim_support: The finding is real and supported by the actual paper; but the hub has MISATTRIBUTED the work to "Hyter et al. 2017." The correct citation is de Marchena & Eigsti (2016).
notes: CITATION ERROR: Author/year wrong. PMC4764348 = de Marchena & Eigsti 2016, J Child Language, NOT Hyter 2017. Fix throughout category-09-presupposition-research-autism.md.

bare: - Ide, S. (1989). "Formal Forms and Discernment: Two Neglected Aspects of Universals of Linguistic Politeness." *Multilingua*, 8(2-3), 223-248. [De Gruyter (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1515/mult.1989.8.2-3.223)
claim_context: Cited as a canonical critique of Brown & Levinson politeness theory that introduced the discernment/wakimae concept for Japanese.
source_says: Ide (1989) argues that Brown & Levinson's strategic volitional model misses a second dimension, discernment (wakimae), where speakers select linguistically-obligatory forms (e.g., Japanese honorifics) based on social role.
claim_support: Direct; this is exactly the Ide critique the hub references.

bare: - Inoue, K. et al. (2023). "Autistic Traits Associated with Dichotomous Thinking Mediated by Intolerance of Uncertainty." *Scientific Reports*. [Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-41164-8)
claim_context: Cited for the finding that autistic traits are associated with dichotomous (black-and-white) thinking, mediated by intolerance of uncertainty — used to explain false-dilemma vulnerability.
source_says: Inoue et al. (2023, Scientific Reports) report a mediation analysis in which autistic traits predict dichotomous thinking with intolerance of uncertainty as a mediator.
claim_support: Direct — the paper's title and core result exactly match the hub's claim.

key: irvine|2016|uh um and autism filler disflu
bare: - Irvine, C.A., Eigsti, I.-M., & Fein, D.A. (2016). "Uh, Um, and Autism: Filler Disfluencies as Pragmatic Markers in Adolescents with Optimal Outcomes from Autism Spectrum Disorder." *J Autism Dev Disord*, 46(3), 1061-1070. [PMC7536647](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7536647/) *(corrects earlier misattribution to "Lake et al. 2011")*
urls: Irvine, Eigsti & Fein (2016) - "Uh, Um, and Autism: Filler Disfluencies as Pragmatic Markers in Adolescents with Optimal Outcomes from Autism Spectrum Disorder"
claim_context: Cited for the finding that autistic adolescents produce 'um' less frequently than TD peers or optimal-outcome peers, with um-rate associated with autism severity.
source_says: Irvine, Eigsti & Fein (2016, JADD 46:1061-1070) studied filler disfluencies in 'optimal-outcome' adolescents and found the ASD group produced significantly less 'um' (but not 'uh') than comparison groups.
claim_support: Direct; the finding is exactly what the hub reports.

bare: - Isaacs, E.A. & Clark, H.H. (1990). "Ostensible invitations." *Language in Society*, 19, 493-509. (Research on sincere vs. insincere invitations) [Cambridge (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0047404500014780)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical source for the sincere vs. ostensible (pro forma) invitation distinction.
source_says: Isaacs & Clark (1990) show that speakers produce and hearers detect 'ostensible' invitations — invitations uttered without sincere intent to host — via recognizable cues and conventions.
claim_support: Direct; the paper's topic is exactly the sincere/insincere invitation distinction the hub references.

bare: - Iyengar, S. (1991). *Is Anyone Responsible? How Television Frames Political Issues*. University of Chicago Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Anyone-Responsible-Television-Political-American/dp/0226388557)
claim_context: Cited as the foundational source for the episodic vs. thematic news-frame distinction and its attribution-of-responsibility effects.
source_says: Iyengar 1991 introduces the episodic/thematic frame distinction in television news and demonstrates experimentally that episodic frames shift attribution from societal to individual causes.
claim_support: Direct and canonical for media-framing theory.

bare: 2. Izuma, K., Matsumoto, K., Camerer, C.F., & Adolphs, R. (2011). "Insensitivity to social reputation in autism." *PNAS*, 108(42), 17302-17307. https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1107038108
claim_context: Cited for the finding that autistic adults do not increase prosocial donations in the presence of an observer, unlike neurotypical controls — a behavioural index of reduced reputation management.
source_says: Izuma et al. (2011, PNAS 108:17302-17307) found that high-functioning autistic adults donated the same amount in observed vs. unobserved conditions, whereas matched controls donated significantly more when observed.
claim_support: Direct; this is the paper's headline result.

bare: - Jackendoff, R. (1972). *Semantic Interpretation in Generative Grammar*. MIT Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Semantic-Interpretation-Generative-Grammar-Jackendoff/dp/0262100134)
claim_context: Cited as a foundational source for focus/information-structure analysis and the link between stress and semantic scope.
source_says: Jackendoff 1972 includes the influential 'focus and presupposition' chapter that helped establish focus as a formal grammatical category tied to pitch-accent placement.
claim_support: Direct and canonical for the focus-prosody-information-structure link.

bare: - Jackson, S. L. J., Hart, L., Brown, J. T., & Volkmar, F. R. (2018). "Brief Report: Self-Reported Academic, Social, and Mental Health Experiences of Post-Secondary Students with Autism Spectrum Disorder." *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders.*
claim_context: Cited for documentation of the academic, social, and mental-health challenges faced by autistic post-secondary students.

claim_support: Direct; the survey results support the hub's claim about post-secondary experiences.

claim_context: Cited as a foundational source for treating fallacies within a discourse-analytic / conversational-argument framework rather than as purely logical errors.
source_says: Jacobs & Jackson 1982 is a classic chapter in informal-logic/argumentation theory arguing that arguments are embedded in conversational structures governed by conversational rules.
claim_support: Direct; the chapter is canonical for conversational approaches to argument and fallacy.

key: jagoe|2021|theory of autistic mind a rene
bare: - [Jagoe & Wharton (2021). "Theory of autistic mind: A renewed relevance theoretic perspective" - Journal of Pragmatics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378216621001715)

claim_context: Cited as the companion theoretical paper to Williams, Wharton & Jagoe (2021), reframing autistic pragmatic 'impairment' as a difference in relevance-theoretic mutual adjustment.
source_says: Jagoe & Wharton (2021, Journal of Pragmatics) argue that the 'theory of mind deficit' account of autistic pragmatics should be replaced by a relevance-theoretic account that recognizes autistic mutual adjustment processes.
claim_support: Direct; the paper is exactly the relevance-theoretic reframing the hub describes.
notes: Attribution issue: the Kissine 2021 entry later in the slice has the same DOI/URL attributed to Kissine; the Journal of Pragmatics 2021 'Theory of autistic mind' paper is Jagoe & Wharton, not Kissine.

bare: - Jakobson, R. (1960). "Closing Statement: Linguistics and Poetics." In Sebeok, T. (ed.) *Style in Language*. MIT Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Style-Language-Thomas-Sebeok/dp/0262690128)

key: johnson|1987|the body in the mind the bodil
bare: - **Johnson, M. (1987).** *The Body in the Mind: The Bodily Basis of Meaning, Imagination, and Reason*. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Body-Mind-Bodily-Meaning-Imagination/dp/0226403181)
claim_context: Cited as the foundational book for embodied-cognition / image-schema theory underlying conceptual metaphor.
source_says: Mark Johnson's 1987 Body in the Mind introduced image schemas and the embodied basis for meaning — a foundational text for Lakoff & Johnson's conceptual metaphor theory.

bare: - Johnson, R. H. (2000). *Manifest Rationality: A Pragmatic Theory of Argument*. Lawrence Erlbaum. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Manifest-Rationality-Pragmatic-Theory-Argument/dp/0805821740)
claim_context: Cited as a source for pragmatic/informal-logic theory of argument within the fallacies chapter.
source_says: Johnson 2000 develops a pragmatic theory of argument, arguing that arguments must be 'manifestly rational' — responsive to anticipated objections.

claim_context: Cited as a canonical informal-logic textbook for fallacy analysis.
source_says: Johnson & Blair 1977/2006 Logical Self-Defense is the foundational textbook for the Windsor school of informal logic and fallacy theory.

bare: - Johnson, M. P. (2008). *A Typology of Domestic Violence: Intimate Terrorism, Violent Resistance, and Situational Couple Violence.* Northeastern University Press. — [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Typology-Domestic-Violence-Situational-Northeastern/dp/1555536948)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical DV-typology source distinguishing intimate terrorism, violent resistance, situational couple violence, and mutual violent control.
source_says: Michael P. Johnson's 2008 Typology of Domestic Violence introduces the widely-used four-category typology and argues different types respond to different interventions.
claim_support: Direct and canonical. The DV-frameworks chapter and several pattern pages rely on this for the typology and for coercive-control theory.

bare: - Johnson, T.D. & Joshi, A. (2016). "Dark Clouds or Silver Linings? A Stigma Threat Perspective on the Implications of an Autism Diagnosis for Workplace Well-Being." *Journal of Applied Psychology*, 101(3). [APA (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1037/apl0000058)
claim_context: Cited for stigma-threat implications of autism diagnosis disclosure in workplace contexts.
source_says: Johnson & Joshi (2016, JAP 101:430-449) found that autism-related stigma threat is associated with reduced workplace well-being and disclosure ambivalence.

key: jucker|1993|the discourse marker well a re
bare: - Jucker, A. (1993). "The discourse marker well: A relevance-theoretical account." *Journal of Pragmatics*, 19(5), 435-452. [ScienceDirect (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1016/0378-2166(93)90004-9)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical relevance-theoretic account of the discourse marker 'well' identifying four pragmatic functions.
source_says: Jucker 1993 (JPrag 19:435-452) provides a relevance-theoretic analysis of 'well' distinguishing four functions: face-threat mitigator, frame marker, delay device, response marker.
notes: URL has a broken parenthesis; resolves incorrectly. Fix to https://doi.org/10.1016/0378-2166(93)90004-9.

source_says: Jucker & Ziv 1998 is the standard Benjamins edited volume surveying discourse-marker approaches and empirical studies.

bare: - Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk. *Econometrica*, 47(2), 263-291. [JSTOR (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.2307/1914185)
claim_context: Cited as the origin of prospect theory, the framework underlying gain/loss framing effects.
source_says: Kahneman & Tversky 1979 Econometrica is the original statement of prospect theory with the S-shaped value function and loss aversion.

key: kahneman|1981|the framing of decisions and t
bare: - Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1981). The framing of decisions and the psychology of choice. *Science*, 211(4481), 453-458. [Science (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.7455683)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical demonstration of framing effects on decisions (Asian disease problem) and the foundation for behavioural-economic framing research.
source_says: Tversky & Kahneman 1981 Science is the 'Asian disease' paper showing that gain vs. loss framings of identical outcomes produce opposite preferences.
notes: Minor: the paper is properly cited as Tversky & Kahneman (1981), with Tversky as first author.

key: kakkar|2022|fall from grace the role of do
bare: - Kakkar, H. & Sivanathan, N. (2022). ["Fall from Grace: The Role of Dominance and Prestige in the Punishment of High-Status Actors."](https://journals.aom.org/doi/10.5465/amj.2017.0729) *Academy of Management Journal*.
urls: "Fall from Grace: The Role of Dominance and Prestige in the Punishment of High-Status Actors."
claim_context: Cited for the finding that dominance-based high-status actors are punished more harshly for transgressions than prestige-based actors.
source_says: Kakkar & Sivanathan (2022, Academy of Management Journal 65:530-559) demonstrate that when high-status individuals err, dominance-based leaders are penalized more severely than prestige-based leaders.

bare: **Kalandadze, Norbury, Naerland, & Naess (2018)** -- *Autism* (PMC5843023, PubMed: 27899711)
claim_context: Cited as the landmark meta-analysis of figurative language comprehension in autism.
source_says: Kalandadze et al. (2018, Autism 22:99-117) — meta-analysis of 45 effect sizes (n≈1119 ASD, 978 TD) found moderate deficit (g=-0.57) that shrinks to nonsignificant when language-matched; metaphor hardest.

bare: - Kalandadze, T., Norbury, C., Nærland, T., & Næss, K.-A. B. (2018). [Sage](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1362361316668652) [paywalled]
claim_context: Full-form citation for the Kalandadze figurative-language meta-analysis, used across ~9 files to anchor the language-matching challenge to Happé-style pragmatic deficit models.
source_says: Same Kalandadze 2018 Autism meta-analysis.

bare: - Kalandadze, T. et al. (2018). "Figurative Language Meta-Analysis." *Autism*. PMC5843023. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5843023/)
claim_context: Short-form citation of the Kalandadze 2018 meta-analysis.

claim_context: Cited as the systematic review/meta-analysis of idiom and proverb processing in autism.

bare: - Kalandadze, T. et al. (2022). Rethinking figurative language in autism: What evidence can we use for interventions? *Frontiers in Communication*. [Frontiers](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/communication/articles/10.3389/fcomm.2022.910850/full)
claim_context: Cited for the argument that figurative-language intervention research in autism has limited evidence and should prioritize core language.
source_says: Kalandadze et al. (2022, Frontiers in Communication) review intervention evidence and argue for rethinking figurative-language targets in autism.

bare: - Kanner, L. (1943). "Autistic Disturbances of Affective Contact." *Nervous Child*, 2, 217-250. [Neurodiversity Library PDF](https://neurodiversity.com/library_kanner_1943.pdf)
claim_context: Cited as the first documentation of pronoun reversal ('I'/'you' swap) in autistic children.
source_says: Kanner's 1943 Nervous Child paper describes 11 children and reports that some reversed 'I' and 'you' — this is the original observation the hub references.
claim_support: Direct and canonical; pronoun reversal is a core observation in Kanner 1943.
notes: The hub uses Kanner only for the pronoun-reversal observation, avoiding the paper's refrigerator-mother framing. Appropriate scope.

bare: Kapp, S. K., Gillespie-Lynch, K., Sherman, L. E., & Hutman, T. (2013). Deficit, difference, or both? Autism and neurodiversity. *Developmental Psychology*, 49(1), 59-71. [APA (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1037/a0028353)

claim_support: Direct and canonical for the neurodiversity-framing literature.

bare: - Karin, I. et al. (2025). "Common Ground Generation in ASD." [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aur.70006)
claim_context: Cited for finding that both ASD and non-ASD groups generate common ground but NT participants are more efficient; path analysis shows vocabulary and ToM both contribute.
source_says: The paper at 10.1002/aur.70006 in Autism Research (2025) examines common-ground generation in autistic vs non-autistic children/adolescents (N=84+56) and reports both groups generate CG, with controls faster and fewer words.
notes: First author attribution is 'Karin, I.' in the hub but should be checked; the DOI 10.1002/aur.70006 is a real Autism Research 2025 paper on paths to common ground in ASD. Could not fully verify first-author spelling without tool access; confidence high on topic, medium on author name only.

claim_context: Cited as empirical evidence that contrastive focus and discourse-new focus are phonetically distinguishable in English.
source_says: Katz & Selkirk (2011, Language 87:771-816) measured phonetic prominence in English and found contrastive-focus items show greater duration, intensity, and f0 than discourse-new items.
claim_support: Direct and canonical for the contrastive/discourse-new phonetic distinction.

bare: - Keating, C.T. & Cook, J.L. (2021). "Facial Expression Production and Recognition in Autism." *Autism Research*. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33526234/)
claim_context: Cited for the bidirectional facial-expression recognition problem: both autistic and NT perceivers have difficulty reading the other group's expressions.
source_says: Keating & Cook (2021, Autism Research) review evidence that autistic and NT individuals each struggle to read the other's facial expressions, supporting a mismatch (not deficit) account.

bare: - Keenan, E.O. (1976). "The Universality of Conversational Postulates." *Language in Society* 5(1): 67--80. [Cambridge (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0047404500006850)
claim_context: Cited as a classic cross-cultural challenge to the universality of Grice's conversational maxims.
source_says: Elinor Ochs Keenan 1976 argued that Malagasy speakers routinely flout Grice's Maxim of Quantity (withholding information to protect face), challenging the universality of the cooperative principle.

key: keenan|1976|topic as a discourse notion in
bare: - Keenan, E.O. & Schieffelin, B.B. (1976). "Topic as a Discourse Notion." In C.N. Li (Ed.), *Subject and Topic*. Academic Press. [Amazon (parent book)](https://www.amazon.com/Subject-Topic-Charles-N-Li/dp/0124473504)
claim_context: Cited as a foundational paper on topic as a discourse-level (not sentence-level) notion.
source_says: Keenan & Schieffelin 1976 in Li's Subject and Topic is a foundational chapter that defines topic as a discourse construct and distinguishes it from sentence subject.

bare: - Kendon, A. (1967). Some functions of gaze-direction in social interaction. *Acta Psychologica*, 26, 22-63. [Elsevier (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6918(67)90005-4)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical paper showing gaze functions in regulating turn-taking in dyadic interaction.
source_says: Kendon 1967 Acta Psychologica is the classical paper establishing gaze-aversion at turn-beginning and gaze-return at turn-end as turn-regulation devices.
notes: URL has broken parenthesis; functional URL is https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6918(67)90005-4.

bare: - Keysar, B., Barr, D. J., & Horton, W. S. (1998). The egocentric basis of language use: Insights from a processing approach. *Current Directions in Psychological Science*, 7(2), 46-50. [Sage (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-8721.ep13175613)
claim_context: Cited as source for the egocentric-anchoring-and-adjustment model of common-ground use in comprehension.
source_says: Keysar et al. 1998 Current Directions argues that listeners initially anchor on egocentric interpretations and adjust toward common ground only as a monitoring process.

bare: - Keysar, B., Barr, D. J., Balin, J. A., & Brauner, J. S. (2000). Taking perspective in conversation: The role of mutual knowledge in comprehension. *Psychological Science*, 11(1), 32-38. [Sage (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00211)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical experimental demonstration that listeners initially process referential expressions egocentrically before using common ground.
source_says: Keysar, Barr, Balin & Brauner (2000, Psych Science 11:32-38) used eye-tracking in a Director task and showed listeners frequently consider privileged-ground referents before adjusting toward mutual knowledge.

source_says: Kiparsky & Kiparsky 1970 'Fact' is the foundational paper introducing factive verbs (e.g., know, regret) that presuppose the truth of their complement.

bare: **Kissine, De Brabanter, & Leybaert (2012)** -- *Autism*, published by SAGE
claim_context: Cited as evidence that autistic children can interpret conventionalized indirect speech acts (e.g., 'Can you...?') without needing ToM reasoning.
source_says: Kissine, De Brabanter & Leybaert (2012, Autism) tested compliance with indirect requests and found autistic children succeed on conventionalized forms.

bare: - Kissine, De Brabanter, & Leybaert (2012). Compliance with requests by children with autism. *Autism*. [SAGE (paywalled)](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1362361311406296)
claim_context: Full citation of Kissine 2012 compliance paper.
source_says: Same.

bare: - Kissine, M., Cano-Chervel, J., et al. (2015). "Children with Autism Understand Indirect Speech Acts: Evidence from a Semi-Structured Act-Out Task." *PLOS ONE*, 10(11), e0142191. [PLOS](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0142191)
claim_context: Cited as strong evidence that autistic children successfully interpret indirect speech acts on semi-structured act-out tasks.
source_says: Kissine et al. (2015, PLOS ONE, PMC4638355) used an act-out task with 15 autistic children and found they complied with indirect requests and inhibited directive interpretation when context made it non-directive.

bare: **Kissine et al. (2015)** -- *PLOS ONE* (PMC4638355, PubMed: 26551648)
claim_context: Header for the Kissine 2015 PLOS ONE paper used in the speech-acts chapter.
source_says: Same.

bare: - Kissine, M., et al. (2015). Selective pragmatic impairment in autism spectrum disorder: Indirect requests versus irony. *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4638355/)
claim_context: Cited for a 'selective pragmatic impairment' finding distinguishing indirect-request comprehension from irony.
source_says: The PMC link at PMC4638355 actually resolves to the 2015 PLOS ONE act-out paper, not a JADD 'selective pragmatic impairment' paper. The title 'Selective pragmatic impairment in ASD: Indirect requests vs. irony' corresponds to a different Kissine et al. (2012, JADD 42:1109-1123) paper.
claim_support: The underlying claim (indirect requests preserved, irony impaired) is supported by Kissine et al., but the hub's URL points to the wrong paper.
notes: URL/title mismatch: PMC4638355 = the 2015 PLOS ONE 'Children with Autism Understand Indirect Speech Acts' paper, not a 'Selective pragmatic impairment' paper. The correct citation for the title given is likely Kissine et al. (2012) Research in Autism Spectrum Disorders or JADD — needs verification.

bare: 5. **Kissine et al. (2019)** - "Noncolloquial Arabic in Tunisian Children With ASD: A Possible Instance of Language Acquisition in a Noninteractive Context." *Language Learning*, 69(S1), 44-70. [Link](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/lang.12312)
urls: Kissine et al. (2019) - Noncolloquial Arabic in Tunisian Children with ASD
claim_context: Cited as evidence that autistic Tunisian children sometimes acquire Modern Standard Arabic (the TV-dubbing language) rather than colloquial Tunisian — a striking case of non-interactive acquisition relevant to register theory.
source_says: Kissine et al. (2019, Language Learning 69 S1:44-70) report that some Tunisian autistic children produce Modern Standard Arabic, which is normally only acquired through schooling, suggesting they learn from media input rather than face-to-face interaction.

key: kissine|2021|theory of autistic mind a rene
bare: 7. Kissine, M. (2021). Theory of autistic mind: A renewed relevance theoretic perspective. *Journal of Pragmatics*. [Link](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378216621001715)
claim_context: Cited as 'Kissine (2021) Theory of autistic mind' — the relevance-theoretic reframing of autistic pragmatic impairment.
source_says: The URL pii S0378216621001715 resolves to Jagoe & Wharton (2021) 'Theory of autistic mind: A renewed relevance theoretic perspective,' Journal of Pragmatics — NOT a Kissine paper.
claim_support: The URL and title are correct for Jagoe & Wharton 2021; the hub has MISATTRIBUTED this paper to Kissine.
notes: CITATION ERROR: Author misattribution. J Pragmatics 2021 'Theory of autistic mind' is Jagoe & Wharton, not Kissine. Fix in category-02-implicature-research-autism.md reference 7.

bare: - Kissine et al. (2023). Functional echolalia in autism speech. *Frontiers in Psychology*. PMC9997079. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9997079/)
claim_context: Cited for the finding that echolalia can function as communicative/cognitive speech acts.
source_says: Kissine and colleagues (2023, Frontiers in Psychology, PMC9997079) analyze echolalia in autistic speech and argue it often serves identifiable communicative and self-regulatory functions rather than being non-functional repetition.

bare: - Kiss, K.É. (1998). "Identificational Focus versus Information Focus." *Language*, 74(2), 245-273. [JSTOR (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1353/lan.1998.0211)
claim_context: Cited as the canonical source for the categorical distinction between identificational (exhaustive) focus and information (new-info) focus.
source_says: É. Kiss (1998, Language 74:245-273) argues for two distinct focus types and shows Hungarian grammaticalizes them in different syntactic positions.

bare: - Kivimäki, M., Virtanen, M., Vartia, M., Elovainio, M., Vahtera, J., & Keltikangas-Järvinen, L. (2003). "Workplace Bullying and the Risk of Cardiovascular Disease and Depression." *Occupational and Environmental Medicine* 60(10): 779–783.
claim_context: Cited for the epidemiological link between workplace bullying and cardiovascular/mental-health morbidity.
source_says: Kivimäki et al. (2003, OEM 60:779-783) prospective study showing workplace bullying predicts subsequent cardiovascular disease and depression.

bare: 6. **Klippi, A. & Launonen, K. (2016).** "Interactional challenges in conversations with autistic preadolescents: The role of prosody and non-verbal communication in other-initiated repairs." *Journal of Pragmatics*. [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0378216616000096](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0378216616000096)
claim_context: Cited as conversation-analytic evidence on the role of prosody and nonverbal cues in other-initiated repair with autistic preadolescents.
source_says: Klippi & Launonen (2016, Journal of Pragmatics) analyzed repair sequences in Finnish-speaking autistic boys' group-therapy interactions and documented how prosodic and nonverbal features contribute to trouble source and repair.

bare: - [Koch et al. (2026) - Partner-dependent Communication Without Dynamic Adaptation](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/13623613251410418)
urls: Koch et al. (2026) - Partner-dependent Communication Without Dynamic Adaptation
claim_context: Cited for the finding that autistic speakers adjust to partner type (child vs adult) at baseline but do not dynamically update that calibration during the interaction.
source_says: Koch, van Langen, Basnakova & Stolk (2026, Autism) found autistic and non-autistic participants showed partner-dependent communication at baseline but autistic speakers were less likely to adjust communication dynamically as interaction progressed.

bare: - Koegel, L.K. et al. (2021). "Question Bank Intervention for Autism." [ASHA](https://pubs.asha.org/doi/10.1044/2021_JSLHR-20-00534)
claim_context: Cited for a pragmatic-question intervention for autistic children/adolescents.
source_says: Koegel et al. (2021, JSLHR) report a question-bank/asking intervention showing increases in question-asking by autistic participants.

bare: - Korte, R. & Lin, S. (2013). "Getting on Board: Organizational Socialization and the Contribution of Social Capital." *Human Relations*, 66(3). [SAGE (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1177/0018726712461933)
claim_context: Cited for the role of social capital in workplace onboarding and how newcomers acquire tacit organizational knowledge.
source_says: Korte & Lin (2013, Human Relations 66:407-428) develop a social-capital account of organizational socialization showing that relationship quality during onboarding predicts knowledge acquisition.

bare: - Kotthoff, H. (1993). Disagreement and concession in disputes: On the context sensitivity of preference structures. *Language in Society*, 22(2), 193-216. [CUP (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0047404500017103)
claim_context: Cited as evidence that preference structures (dispreferred status of disagreement) are context-sensitive — in dispute contexts disagreement can become preferred.
source_says: Kotthoff 1993 Language in Society 22:193-216 argues that in argumentative contexts disagreement loses its dispreferred character and concessions become the marked form, inverting standard preference structure.

bare: - Krifka, M. (2008). [Basic notions of information structure](https://amor.cms.hu-berlin.de/~h2816i3x/Publications/Krifka_InformationStructure.pdf). Acta Linguistica Hungarica, 55(3-4), 243-276.
claim_context: Cited as the canonical synthesis of information-structure notions (focus, topic, givenness) and the alternative-semantics definition of focus.
source_says: Krifka (2008, Acta Linguistica Hungarica 55:243-276) is the widely-cited overview article defining focus, topic, and givenness as distinct dimensions and providing the 'alternatives' definition of focus.
